segments.title: Hermes makes the small-carrier prompt plausible
segments.summary: The opening turns a ten-carrier patron prompt into a design question about whether Hermes could be brought under the treaty's 10,000-ton line.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke starts by asking how Britain could plausibly build a swarm of sub-10,000-ton Hermes-style carriers between 1926 and 1930. He points to Hermes's heavy 5.5-inch gun battery, armor, machinery, and older construction choices as weight that a follow-on design could trim. The value of the moment is that it treats the scenario as naval architecture and treaty practice, not just a wish for more decks.

evidence.note: Introduces the prompt about ten small Hermes carriers and says a historically viable option exists.
Lists Hermes's displacement, guns, armor, machinery, and possible weight-saving changes.

segments.title: Washington Treaty tonnage favors smaller carrier numbers
segments.summary: The treaty section explains why total carrier tonnage, experimental-carrier replacement rules, and the 10,000-ton warship threshold make small carriers attractive.
segments.body: This watch point lays out the legal machinery behind the scenario. The Washington Naval Treaty gives Britain 135,000 tons of carrier allowance, but a fleet of 27,000-ton carriers would leave too few ships for global operations and maintenance cycles. Hermes also falls into the experimental-carrier replacement logic, while ships at or below about 10,000 tons avoid the later cruiser-style cumulative limits that would make a cruiser-carrier category harder to exploit.

evidence.note: Explains the 135,000-ton carrier allowance and why five large carriers would not be enough.
Uses Article 8 to show that Hermes and other British carriers counted as experimental and could be replaced.
Explains the lack of cumulative limits for ships at or below the 10,000-ton threshold.

segments.title: Hermes and County-class features shape a cruiser-carrier design
segments.summary: The design discussion compares Hermes, Colossus, and County-class dimensions to show how Britain might make a useful small carrier.
segments.body: The design section checks the idea against real hulls, machinery, fuel, flight-deck length, air group limits, and seakeeping. Dr. Clarke treats Hermes as a good starting point but not a finished pattern: later boilers, fewer guns, simpler topweight, and a County-class-style hull could produce a stronger cruiser carrier. The comparison matters because it shows how a small carrier could support cruiser reconnaissance without needing fleet-carrier speed or size.

evidence.note: Reviews Hermes's displacement, dimensions, machinery, speed, range, and possible light-fleet-carrier significance.
Identifies first-generation carrier design features that could be changed in later small carriers.
Uses the County-class hull and machinery comparison to describe a plausible cruiser-carrier design route.

segments.title: Beatty and Fleet Air Arm politics explain why the route was missed
segments.summary: The episode links the absence of a Hermes follow-on class to Beatty's conservatism, RAF control of naval aviation, and Britain's interwar budget assumptions.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that the small-carrier path runs into institutional politics as much as design feasibility. Beatty's confidence that the Royal Air Force could be made to meet naval needs, along with his caution about provoking another naval race, helps explain why the Royal Navy did not push the carrier-cruiser idea harder. The same passage also frames Britain's wider gamble: paper parity with the United States mattered less if Congress would not fund the US Navy to match the Royal Navy in practice.

evidence.note: Connects the missed small-carrier route to Beatty and the transfer of naval aviation to the Royal Air Force.
Explains Beatty's procurement caution and the Royal Navy's assumption that America would not fully fund treaty parity.

segments.title: Cruiser carriers spread aviation across imperial stations
segments.summary: The deployment model attaches cruiser carriers to squadrons on the China, East Indies, South Atlantic, and home stations for trade protection and presence.
segments.body: This section turns the extra ships into an operating pattern. Rather than concentrating every carrier with the battle fleet, Dr. Clarke imagines cruiser-carrier pairs or divisions working with cruiser squadrons on distant stations. The payoff is practical: Britain gains scouting, trade-protection reach, and forward aviation in places where a few fleet carriers based at home or in the Mediterranean cannot always cover.

evidence.note: Describes attaching small carriers to cruiser squadrons on China, East Indies, South Atlantic, and related stations.
Explains how station coverage, refit cycles, and Mediterranean air-threat assumptions shape the deployment model.

segments.title: A light-carrier treaty category changes the building plan
segments.summary: The scenario shows how more cruiser-size carriers could sustain shipyard work, draw in Australia, and lead to a 13,500-ton light-carrier category.
segments.body: The middle of the episode follows the industrial and treaty consequences of normalizing small carriers. More cruiser-size hulls would give British yards extra work alongside Counties and Nelsons, with Australia and possibly New Zealand helping expand the imperial carrier force. Dr. Clarke then proposes a later light-carrier category around 13,500 tons, which would let navies preserve fleet-carrier tonnage while using lighter carriers for presence, reconnaissance, and station work.

evidence.note: Explains the added yard work, County-class interaction, and possible Australian or New Zealand involvement.
Suggests a light carrier category built around 13,500 tons and separate cumulative limits.
Works through British and Australian carrier numbers and forward-station deployment.

segments.title: Japan, America, France, and Italy all get reasons to study cruiser carriers
segments.summary: The international section explains why British small carriers could push other navies toward reconnaissance, amphibious support, or regional-presence carriers.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke broadens the scenario beyond Britain by asking how rival and allied navies would react to a working cruiser-carrier pattern. The United States has reconnaissance and fleet-exercise reasons to experiment, while Japan could adapt the idea for numbers, scouting, amphibious support, and offsetting larger powers. France and Italy also become candidates because smaller carriers fit their tighter carrier allowances and regional commitments better than expensive full-size fleet carriers.

evidence.note: Discusses Japanese and American reasons to look at British cruiser-carrier practice.
Connects Japanese carrier interest to offset strategy, amphibious operations, and spotting roles.
Adds French and Italian incentives for smaller carriers under limited carrier allowances.

segments.title: Small carriers become laboratories for naval aviation
segments.summary: The later design section argues that repeated small-carrier construction could accelerate deck-edge lifts, flight-deck strength choices, and fleet-carrier lessons.
segments.body: This watch point is useful for understanding how hardware learning happens before computer simulation. Dr. Clarke argues that building more small carriers would give navies more chances to test ideas such as deck-edge lifts, flight-deck arrangements, armored-deck options, and aviation support ships. Because a small carrier is one of many rather than one of a handful of fleet carriers, it can absorb more design risk and feed lessons into the larger ships.

evidence.note: Explains how extra light-carrier tonnage could change Ark Royal, Ranger, Wasp, and Yorktown-style decisions.
Uses deck-edge lift development and British experience to show how carrier technology could advance through testing.
States that smaller carriers would likely carry more design experimentation than scarce fleet carriers.

segments.title: Denser naval aviation changes early Second World War problems
segments.summary: The conclusion asks how more light carriers and pilots would change 1930s exercises, Southeast Asian defenses, raider hunts, and early wartime carrier losses.
segments.body: The final substantive section tests the scenario against wartime consequences without pretending the whole war can be cleanly rerun. More carriers mean more pilots, more exercises, and earlier evidence about air attack and air defence, especially around capital ships and cruisers. Dr. Clarke then points to Southeast Asia, the River Plate, Deutschland, Courageous, Glorious, and Norway as examples where additional light carriers might change the choices available to British and Japanese commanders.

evidence.note: Discusses Japanese infrastructure, carrier numbers, pilot recruitment, and training depth.
Asks how more naval aviation would affect 1930s exercises, Southeast Asia, light-carrier defenses, and air attack assumptions.
Uses River Plate, Deutschland, Courageous, Glorious, and Norway as examples of changed wartime carrier employment.
